evals.prompt: Price 1,200, variable cost 780, fixed costs 8,400,000/year. How many units to break even, and how many for 2,100,000 after-tax profit at 30% tax?
evals.expected_output: Contribution 420/unit. Breakeven 20,000 units (24,000,000 revenue). After-tax target needs pre-tax 3,000,000 -> (8,400,000+3,000,000)/420 = 27,143 units.
assertions: Grosses up the after-tax target to 3,000,000 pre-tax

evals.prompt: We're at 65% capacity. A one-off export order: 8,000 units at 450 when our full cost is 520 (variable 380, allocated fixed 140). Extra export packaging 25/unit. Take it?
evals.expected_output: Incremental analysis: contribution = (450-380-25) x 8,000 = 360,000 positive -> accept, given spare capacity and no domestic price spoilage. Allocated fixed cost is irrelevant.
assertions: Ignores allocated fixed cost in the decision
Conditions acceptance on spare capacity and market separation

evals.prompt: Sales wants a 7% price cut claiming volume will rise 12%. CM ratio is 32%. Does the math work?
evals.expected_output: Required volume increase to hold contribution = 7/(32-7) = 28%. The promised 12% falls far short -> contribution falls. Show the calculation and the elasticity the plan implicitly assumes.
assertions: Computes required volume growth of 28% using the CM-ratio formula
Concludes 12% is insufficient and contribution falls
Quantifies the contribution loss at the promised 12%
Frames the burden of proof on demonstrated elasticity

evals.prompt: From the attached unit economics (evals/files/product_economics.csv): compute breakeven units and sales, units needed for the target profit, margin of safety at current volume, and degree of operating leverage.
evals.expected_output: Breakeven 6,000 units ($300,000); target requires 8,000 units; MOS 25%; DOL 4.0.
